segments.text: Welcome to Sonar Summit.
Today I'm joined by someone who helped
shape the whole DevOps movement I guess
and who is now actively exploring what
AI native software development looks like
actually in practice and not just from the sofa.
Patrick, we have a very long time
working together. So welcome and
happy to have you on board.
Yeah, thanks for having me, Edgar. And it's
debatable because I do work on the sofa
most of the time, but it is actually indeed
it's been a long time since 2009 with DevOps.
And now AI is just a new thing and that
seems to be kind of probably over my career
the thing like I learned to live with:
the first internet, then the cloud,
the mobile, serverless, you name it.
And yeah, this is now the fun wave to be in,
and the learning is great so fantastic.
Maybe before we start can I do a short
introduction? What are you doing today?
I know we worked a lot on developer education
and we do a lot of things related to AI
and agents. I think something that you were
working on in your daily life is quite
related to harnessing the AI and make it useful.
So why don't we start with you giving us
a bit more on what's your everyday job today.
What do you do?
Sure. Well I would say I have a dual job.
One is to be in the community and not
about broadcasting whatever I think about,
but it's basically learning from the community
and the industry and kind of doing that.
I'm also kind of doing this within the context
of AI native dev community where people
can come together and share their learnings.
I also organize a conference on that
and then while I look in the industry
and get understanding and help people
make sense of the world, I kind of feed that
back into the company I work for at Tesl.
Where we are building something that helps
agents be more efficient. Think of me as
a little bit the person that brings the
industry back to within the organization.
So we're not always heads down into
"oh this is a cool thing." Okay, industry
thinks about it differently. And that's
kind of a little bit my role. So it's great
that what you think and see and advise
you also see happening within a company.
So I like to have both things and do it there.
And context: Tesl is a startup,
a year and a half ago, two years.
We're at that third pivot as it is in the
industry, but we had spec-driven as one of them.
We were really deep on that and right now
it is very much focused on context
and context engineering. We're not selling
you another agent, we're not selling you
something else, but what we try to do is
to make the existing tools and the agentic
tools shine better with optimized context.
That's cool. Something you mentioned is
very related to product that I love:
actually when you build something and then
you see it flourishing in the organization
of your customers or users. This is always
very satisfying. In your recent blog posts
you wrote something super interesting
and I think our whole topic today will be
around this: a context flywheel.
Not limited to this, but what struck me is
you said that a real differentiation isn't
actually the model or programming language
in which your agent is written, but actually
the organizational context. So it's not even
about one file I believe, but I believe
this idea doesn't come from nowhere.
I think you mentioned that you work with
a lot of teams and community and you're
experimenting. So maybe you can give us
more insights of what you're seeing actually
right now in teams who actually actively
use AI coding agents and what they are
struggling with. What are their top
challenges that they have to overcome?
Sure. The perspective that I deliberately
took was of the organizational level.
So if you would think of code actually getting
more and more—getting the agent to write
context—and then the context is both
technical, organizational, team level and so on.
That brings it up that once you kind of start
building this together you get the agents
performing better. And funny enough also
the humans perform better because they have
better documentation. This is something
that is happening in practice. A lot of people,
maybe their first step was "hey the agent's
not behaving as I want. Let me write
something to agent.md or CLAUDE.md."
That's great. Then that becomes maybe more
complex or they need to pull in context
from another repo, a microservice which
they don't have. They have some context.
"Oh let's pull it in." Maybe it's an open source
library, let's pull that context in.
Oh, I have my tickets. So you see all those
pieces get in more and more and it's expanding.
I would say it is a flywheel in a way;
once one team starts with it, the other
kind of gets better at it and then they
get better context so their job gets better.
And eventually that goes into the whole
organization. We can probably come better
back to it later. There is obviously this
on the technology level like I understand
the technical systems better and then you
can even go broader and say I understand
my business context level better.
So on that product level and the technical
level you kind of start building that context
on both sides which is unique to companies
building this step by step.
Yeah that's cool. Something that really
sparked my interest, because I feel the
same when I restart my agent session every
time in the terminal where I have like
three or four, is that you wrote something
around every agent session being like
a new hire. Although the span of this hire
is quite short because context gets exploded
and you feel how it degrades with every cycle.
But then in some other publication that
you did you wrote also about a very interesting
concept called Context Development Life Cycles.
Where you have this Generate, Evaluate,
Distribute and Observe. It's not only
about pouring more documentation up front
and like a life cycle will flourish.
Why did you feel that there's a need to
frame these specific four steps together?
Obviously SDLC was probably one of the
inspirations to that because the letters
are the same plus-minus but it's a different
concept. Can you tell more about why
exactly these steps frame that the best?
Yeah, so first thing on the onboarding
which is funnily enough, we've always
struggled with writing documentation for
people that come in and it got stale.
Now people have an incentive. People are
writing context for the agent. They have
this self-interest of writing it for the
agent so they don't have to do it over again.
So that helps. And once that's written,
that kind of helps people, but also the agents.
Where does it help people? It helps new
people almost like an up-to-date readme.
And it helps the agent with more
up-to-date context. Now as you know,
one of the reasons that documentation
got stale is that the organization
changes all the time. So documentation
on its own has a life cycle. For many
people now changing their CLAUDE.md or
similar, they go in once, they update it,
and then they kind of forget about this
and say "oh it doesn't work anymore."
"What do we do?" Oh, we do like a manual
check and say, "Hey, we changed a few
lines, we changed a few words, we added
something. I think it's okay." This is
actually where the vibe check came from.
Whenever there was a new model, we were
doing a vibe check on "hey, is our context
still operating the way we want it to?"
It had nothing to do initially with vibe
coding and accepting YOLO mode apps. It
was about the check, and the more
engineering-like phrase for this is: when
you have context or prompts you build a test.
The test equivalent within that world would
be called an eval, right? So yes, I'm
writing those tests, writing those evals,
solo in my repo, all good. Now it's
almost like building an app. I build an app,
I write my tests, it all looks green
but we all know that it works on my machine.
It works in my context. So the next step
is typically I want to distribute that,
deploy that like in CI/CD terms to other
people and then I want to make sure they
use it or they don't use it. Sometimes I
want to force the use of some security rules,
some kind of governance guidelines or team guidelines.
And then after that like what you really
want is—I put it now in deployment and
in our DevOps pipeline what we do is we
actually see when things fail. Not just
the intended fails, but we try to cover
that with our evals. But then people using
our context have different results. Why is that?
And so you start a little bit of that
spelunking of logs, spelunking of "why?"
Oh they have a different model. No, they're
using a different coding agent. Maybe the
API has changed that they're referring to,
or we changed the way we do around things.
All these things happen and you see more
and more people looking not just at
"oh it worked for me." They're looking
at logs, they're looking at what the agent
did, they're tracking that and it becomes
an observability tool. So we put it first,
we test it with evals, we distribute it,
deploy it, we see it out in the wild and
then we pick up signals. One of the signals
is if let's say 15 people are developing
and the agent always doesn't know the
answer or it always gives an error, maybe
we should all update that as a central thing.
And that comes back to: hey, if I'm a
platform team or a developer experience team,
maybe I can find the signals across the
teams and then like help them curate the
context across. So that's where the
observability comes in. And then once you
have that like you know we know what to
improve you start in the generate or create
like assisted with a coding agent to update
your CLAUDE.md. You run your evals, you
distribute, you observe. I think the power
of that loop drawn through it is I got
so frustrated that it was like this artisanal
manual thing of optimizing my context.
I just wanted engineering rigor like just
run these tests and then I know and then I
know what to fix. And the time we save
by giving it context... there were times we
totally got lost when it didn't give the
right context. So anyway that was kind of
a lot of that early things. I had prototypes
of Dockerfiles being created but how do
I actually test them? I think slowly we're
adapting this in the industry as a way
to have an engineering loop. Now first of
all we are discovering this in agentic
coding. It exists already in agentic in
general. We're just two years later from
what they're doing but it is now becoming
available in our tools. And that's kind of
also where Tesl tries to not just say,
"Hey, we're distributing a marketplace,"
but we actually give you advice on
optimizing your context and keeping it up to date.
Yeah. Something that you mentioned is
very interesting: the eval. I know a lot
of people in particular who are now building
agents or agentic workflows. There's always
the question where you land on an eval and
is this benchmark better than this benchmark?
What is your take on what is good enough
when you run an eval? When you say like
"okay now it's ready now the beast is gone
and it will produce value?" Remember the
early days the acceptance of tabs was
ridiculously low around 20% and it was
increased to 34. Somehow this whole vibe
about measuring the eval dissolved; people
just say "we're better." Of course models
compare to each other because it's
deterministic. You have a very clear answer,
very clear question. But now if we talk
about coding, we all know that the same
functionality can be written in probably
hundreds of possible ways and they're all correct.
Depends on what you measure: performance,
energy efficiency, memory, or CPU utilization.
We can find even more parameters. What is
from your experience when you have a product,
you have an agent you want to launch it,
how do you treat the eval? Where do you
draw the line where you say it's enough,
let's give it to users let's see how it runs?
Yeah maybe first give the context of
how would an eval work. I have my repo
set up with my coding files, my agent.md
and all that stuff. I give it a prompt
and it does something maybe agentically
coding and it gives a result. So I'm
assessing that loop by giving this input,
this prompt, and I'm judging the output.
Right now there's a lot of eval systems
that say "well I have some mathematical
statistical thing or measuring." They're
kind of missing the business point: your
value is going to be specific. I want
this to be done with that prompt. Often
think of this almost like behavioral-driven.
I have a scenario that I'm describing
how to do a certain thing. Okay, so that
kind of gives you: how do we describe the
scenarios? We could do that like in our
typical tests like "hey we run it it looks
good," maybe a security scan, maybe a test
coverage. Or we could use quite commonly
an LLM as a judge. So we say given this
input, given this prompt, what do you
think? And it gives its judgment.
The problem is it is not deterministic
as our tests used to be. It's not like a
0/1 where you know it actually is true
or not true because maybe today it gives
that result and tomorrow it gives another.
I changed the prompt, five of my previous
things fail and now the new thing succeeds.
It feels a little bit like whack-a-mole like
"what do I do?" And then you kind of
settle into "ah you know this is okay."
Now the trick about evals is not looking
at it like tests but more like error
budgets. I assume X amount of these things
should succeed. Now you put different
error buckets on different risk levels
like these criteria always have to pass.
The other ones are a little bit more nice
to have, vanity metrics, something like that.
So the trick is to treat it more like
monitoring than gating. Now where you
put that line is going to be dependent
on the risk you're taking with certain
functionality and how much effort you want
to go in. No, I'm not going to lie: it's
like saying, "Well, I'm writing code
without tests." And it goes way faster
because "yeah, I don't have to write tests."
Okay, hey, I'm writing context and doing
this with evals. Doing it with evals will
have more work in a way. Yes, we can have
the LLM suggest scenarios and so on, but
we'll still have to say yes/no to "this
makes sense." And when it fails, we also
have to say "this is acceptable." Now in
a business level, this is hard like the
traditional way of working because it's
not the technical person who can say yes/no;
they don't know how critical or riskful
the PM wants to take the product.
Now in coding you might say that if part
of this is generating technical documentation
then the developer can say "yes that's
acceptable." If it's functional, the
business has to say. So finding that
balance... I'd love to say the number is
54 but it's not like that; it's just going
to depend on your case much like error
budgets are: how much risk you're willing to take.
The advice is also to split it up as
the must-haves and the non-must-haves
and to try to limit results to a binary.
Saying it's kind of okay is not really
helpful. It is okay or not okay. Try to
be very specific in your evals on your
criteria and try to make them a binary
because the rest is really hard to manage
There's something very interesting that
you touched: test comparison to test-driven
development. I think you said something
along the lines: if we test code why don't
we actually test context? What does that
look like in reality? Do I just have
static context everywhere and I only
change the prompt? Or I know statistically
if you want to do AB testing you usually
need to minimize to changing one thing at a time.
When you say testing context does it mean
that you keep everything the same and you
only iterate on adding new tools to context
or how do you see that?
Yeah maybe you have one scenario or some
kind of evaluation that you did. Let's say
I have a microservice repository and it
needs to have authentication for example
on these endpoints, or it needs to be
RESTful or some other guidelines I put
in there to do it. When I would iterate
on that, I'm probably writing prompts
and all of a sudden I find something the
model doesn't know anymore, right? Or it
does it wrong. Let me add that to my rules.
And then I do it again and says, "Ah, it
seems to be better." Now, if you do that
manually over time, it's going to be very
tedious. So I want to almost have a test
suite that says "given that input I'm
going to run this again." And that kind
of gives that more change impact analysis.
Now when do I want to run this? I would say
think of it as you have your coding agent
and it has a bunch of inputs when it needs
to get a certain output. So if your code
would change and you have a different
setup or a different readme, it might be
that your context doesn't apply anymore.
They're out of sync. If I change my
dependencies of my code, maybe it does
something different because the constructs
are now different. Anytime you change a
dependency in that pipeline of "this is
the input and this is the output," then
I want to rerun again. Not every test is
that easy to run and some are more costly
than others similar to a unit test and
an end-to-end test. So you have to think
about what can give quick feedback and
then—no pun intended—there's the Sonar
model that says: do the quick tests. If
they fail you get quick feedback, then
go to the next phase of another set of
tests and then go further. That saves you
time instead of running all the tests
all the time. And then when you have a
major deploy you actually go there. I
would advise to still think in two layers
similar to unit test and BDD. I can
iterate on my context just with a couple
of unit tests very fast and then when I
do the full deploy I'm going to run my
That is very true again with context: how
you treat like how much you run. Running
everything all the time will become more
costly. But a quick sanity check is
something. You could do that either in
while you're coding—almost like a TDD
loop—you can do it in your git commit,
you can do it in CI/CD, you can do it later.
It's just a matter of how costly and how long
you want to run on this. Does that make sense?
Yes, so maybe now we can actually connect
the two dots. On one side you say if you
build an agent you have a lot of moving
parts. But if you just change your model,
even changing models can inevitably destroy
your eval because the model was trained
differently, different values, different
weights and so on. And context is still
the king. So it's not actually a life
cycle that has a start and end, more
like a life cycle process. And a flywheel
is a compound advantage you get from
testing repeatedly on the same number of
parameters. So what does it change when
you say "okay we run the flywheel across
multiple iterations?" What is the compound
value you're getting? Can you touch on this?
Yeah. So maybe I'll first touch on if
you're just doing the life cycle, one of the
things you have to care about is you need
to run it multiple times. That kind of
hardens it. Think about flaky tests that
you have to run again for a while. Now
you get certainty and when you're then
offering your context to somebody else—
let's say your microservice is used by
somebody else—they can also use that
context to test and get better at that.
So that's technically compounding. One
user can do it, your team can do it,
people outside of your team can reuse
your context and that goes further.
Initially when I wrote the post I said
"okay this is kind of your moat." Now the
moat isn't actually in the technical
things because somebody else can have the
same cycle, write the same code. The
compounding thing will be in the business
level: we learned what worked and what
didn't work. We learn what our specific
domain knowledge is that goes in there.
The more and the faster we can put things
out there, the better we refine our context,
the better results we get with our agents,
the better our experiments going to be.
So that's the compounding competitive
edge which somebody might copy a snapshot
of, but they don't have that buildup.
The next level is we're getting code to
context, and then code context maybe in
some memory, but ultimately it's going
to be knowledge about your domain and
your company. And that's your accumulation
over time across the teams.
This is actually interesting because I
think you mentioned four AIS: one side
where we get better agent quality, we
have deeper senior expertise where you
have some tools for sharing stuff across
your teams and not just what works on my
machine. Then you have team learning where
whatever I do that helps me produce better
software is also helpful for others. And
how do you share this know-how? Then also
organizational data in alignment. It
feels like if you nail all four, you
almost create some kind of competitive
advantage for accelerating the speed with
which you are developing software.
What is actually the defensiveness of
that? As we see, models are commoditizing,
we're all getting to the right top corner.
Specialized ones are converging because
nobody wants to create a new API or MCP
tool yet. But tested versions of context
become essentially the moat. Why can't
someone building their moat in one company
go to another one and take the context
with them? It's not that he takes a sack
on his shoulder and everything works;
you still have dependency on languages
and frameworks. How do you see this where
AI is not really isolated to one specific company?
In the very beginning in DevOps there was
this post about web operations from Flickr
at the time. If we reduced the number of
milliseconds on the web we got more
revenue. At that time your technology was
definitely your moat. We have that a
little bit right now: "hey I'm doing AI
I can get features out way better." Now
eventually everybody could read all the
techniques used—they're still using them to
do that. It's not that it's impossible.
Now for some reason people stay in different
levels of expertise and different execution
modes. Imagine agents do everything and
I can just hire my agent Edgar and they
would build everything perfectly. It's still
the person that has to steer that agent
in the right direction. And then you'll
probably get closer to what is my
competitive edge: can I build a feature
everybody wants? Can I build something
that a network actually listens to?
Industry stays the same but we're able
to faster execute. Now there is a funny
thing: imagine we could put out our whole
backlog and everything gets built. What we
learned is if you would update your
software every day, your users go crazy.
Absolutely. So it's not like we've solved
all the things. I can't copy solving
the other pieces of the process to be competitive.
I just want to build on that. If we give
agents autonomously to go and act in our
domains and build new features, does the
context remain this last-mile guardrail
that makes them truly autonomous? Where
you give a spec and it builds with
reliable architecture and security in place?
Does context become this living architecture?
Is context really the golden key for
autonomous agentic development?
I would position context more as the
right fuel than the guardrail. If you have
rocket fuel you can go faster than others,
but you still have to steer it. That is
a little bit like companies that didn't
have a good release pipeline and are
jumping into the AI world—they missed a
step and they're struggling with the rigor.
Now the context will enable us to unlock
other things, but it's not the end thing.
We need to know that it needs grooming,
updating, and testing to keep it in good shape.
It's not a static one-off.
This is a good segue. Imagine some company
takes their teams, gives them Cursor,
Windsurf, Claude Code—any agentic tool.
They're watching what you're saying about
context and say "we're not doing any of that."
What are the smallest first steps?
Where should they begin before they go
and roll out all those agentic tools?
Most companies are using agentic coding
as a solo "I do this and maybe I improve
my life." I think the nurture is: can we
land whatever you learn or your agent is
doing somewhere in an instruction file,
rules file, or CLAUDE.md? That's the first
thing I'll look for. If I don't see it there,
I know they're still doing the manual loop.
If you have that and sharing happening—it
could be in your repo or package it to
a marketplace—then you start looking: are
people keeping this up to date so others can reuse?
I would advise developer experience and
platform teams to build a central registry
of context being used by others, so you go
across team, across project, across agents.
It's not that we couldn't do this a year
and a half ago, but the more people get
on there, we realize there needs to be a
better process. Start writing that down.
If it's not working, write a skill. Even
if somebody's skeptical to use AI and says
"it's crap," great. Do you want to be our
skills writer? That teaches the AI to do
it better. You can even get the critics on
board because they have domain knowledge.
They might write it now in wikis, but
where the rubber meets the road is in the
context of the agent. Turning that into
a context-producing flow within the company.
Little steps: from solo write-it-down to
"hey we have it in a repo." Yes, sharing
this across teams. I think it's very
doable and more like: I need a direction
to do this. This is engineering this out
Fantastic. Harnessing agents is not the
job of one person. If you have a platform
team it's great, but if you're a startup
with 10 engineers, it's the job of each of
them to provide and grow the context and
share the skills and tools. I recently
even saw some guys from a squad in Sonar
who built the tools for the agent to
actually generate the context out of
whatever the agent is doing. Something
you touched also: observability and logs,
why specific decisions were made to navigate
the agent for the next cycle. Patrick,
thank you so much for sharing these insights
and thank you everyone for watching. Enjoy
the rest of Sonar Summit. If you want to
share any links or find you?
If you're interested in the blog posts
on the context flywheel and context
development life cycle, you can go to
tesl.io/blog. I'm easy to reach on
LinkedIn. I have my own blog on Jedi.be.
Anything I post or any conference talk
you'll find there. I'm willing to learn
from you.
Fantastic. Thank you again, Patrick. To
everyone else, enjoy the summit and looking
forward for the rest of the day. Perfect.

fullText: Welcome to Sonar Summit. Today I'm joined by someone who helped shape the whole DevOps movement I guess and who is now actively exploring what AI native software development looks like actually in practice and not just from the sofa. Patrick, we have a very long time working together. So welcome and happy to have you on board. Yeah, thanks for having me, Edgar. And it's debatable because I do work on the sofa most of the time, but it is actually indeed it's been a long time since 2009 with DevOps. And now AI is just a new thing and that seems to be kind of probably over my career the thing like I learned to live with: the first internet, then the cloud, the mobile, serverless, you name it. And yeah, this is now the fun wave to be in, and the learning is great so fantastic. Maybe before we start can I do a short introduction? What are you doing today? I know we worked a lot on developer education and we do a lot of things related to AI and agents. I think something that you were working on in your daily life is quite related to harnessing the AI and make it useful. So why don't we start with you giving us a bit more on what's your everyday job today. What do you do? Sure. Well I would say I have a dual job. One is to be in the community and not about broadcasting whatever I think about, but it's basically learning from the community and the industry and kind of doing that. I'm also kind of doing this within the context of AI native dev community where people can come together and share their learnings. I also organize a conference on that and then while I look in the industry and get understanding and help people make sense of the world, I kind of feed that back into the company I work for at Tesl. Where we are building something that helps agents be more efficient. Think of me as a little bit the person that brings the industry back to within the organization. So we're not always heads down into "oh this is a cool thing." Okay, industry thinks about it differently. And that's kind of a little bit my role. So it's great that what you think and see and advise you also see happening within a company. So I like to have both things and do it there. And context: Tesl is a startup, a year and a half ago, two years. We're at that third pivot as it is in the industry, but we had spec-driven as one of them. We were really deep on that and right now it is very much focused on context and context engineering. We're not selling you another agent, we're not selling you something else, but what we try to do is to make the existing tools and the agentic tools shine better with optimized context. That's cool. Something you mentioned is very related to product that I love: actually when you build something and then you see it flourishing in the organization of your customers or users. This is always very satisfying. In your recent blog posts you wrote something super interesting and I think our whole topic today will be around this: a context flywheel. Not limited to this, but what struck me is you said that a real differentiation isn't actually the model or programming language in which your agent is written, but actually the organizational context. So it's not even about one file I believe, but I believe this idea doesn't come from nowhere. I think you mentioned that you work with a lot of teams and community and you're experimenting. So maybe you can give us more insights of what you're seeing actually right now in teams who actually actively use AI coding agents and what they are struggling with. What are their top challenges that they have to overcome? Sure. The perspective that I deliberately took was of the organizational level. So if you would think of code actually getting more and more—getting the agent to write context—and then the context is both technical, organizational, team level and so on. That brings it up that once you kind of start building this together you get the agents performing better. And funny enough also the humans perform better because they have better documentation. This is something that is happening in practice. A lot of people, maybe their first step was "hey the agent's not behaving as I want. Let me write something to agent.md or CLAUDE.md." That's great.
